steps.title: The Forgotten Ingredient
steps.body:
When you were dating, fun was the point. You laughed, teased, flirted, did spontaneous things.

Then life happened. Careers, kids, bills, responsibilities. Somewhere along the way, fun got filed under 'optional' — right after everything else.

But here's the research: couples who play together stay together. Laughter releases oxytocin — the same bonding chemical released during physical intimacy. Shared fun creates shared memories. Teasing and inside jokes build a private world that only the two of you inhabit.

Play isn't a distraction from adult life. It's the antidote to the heaviness of adult life.

You don't have to be serious all the time to be a serious couple.

Play is not the opposite of commitment.
Play is the celebration of commitment.

questions.label:
When's the last time you and your partner did something purely for fun — no agenda, no logistics, no 'we should'?

Think about the early days. What did you do together that made you laugh? What brought lightness?

What's one small, fun thing you could do this week that doesn't require a reservation or a plan?

steps.title: Types of Play in Relationships
steps.body:
Play doesn't have to mean skydiving or a weekend getaway. Play comes in many forms:

Intellectual play — debating something silly, doing a crossword together, sharing hot takes on a movie

Physical play — wrestling, dancing in the kitchen, chasing each other around the house

Verbal play — inside jokes, silly voices, teasing that's affectionate not cutting

Competitive play — a board game, a video game, betting on who can do something faster

Improvisational play — making up stories together, recreating a funny moment, being ridiculous on purpose

The common thread? You're present, you're engaged, and you're not taking yourselves too seriously.

steps.question: Which type of play comes most naturally to you? Which comes most naturally to your partner?
feedback: Intellectual play is great — it keeps your minds engaged together. Just make sure it doesn't turn into arguing.
Physical play is wonderful for connection. Movement together releases endorphins and builds teamwork.
Verbal play builds a private world of shared humor. Just keep it affectionate, not cutting.
Competitive play can be fun — just remember the goal is connection, not winning.

steps.title: Bringing Play Back
steps.body:
You're both exhausted from a long week. The couch is calling. You could scroll your phones in silence, or...

Play option A: 'Let's watch the first five minutes of three terrible movies and rate them.'

Play option B: 'I'll make us weird mocktails with whatever's in the fridge. No googling recipes.'

Play option C: 'Let's each write down the worst marriage advice we've ever received and read them in a serious voice.'

None of these require planning, money, or energy. They just require choosing fun over default.

steps.title: Plan One Playful Moment
steps.prompt:
Write down one specific, low-effort playful thing you can do with your partner in the next 48 hours.

Not 'go on a date.' Something small, spontaneous, and silly.

Examples: make up a new dance move together, recreate a photo from your first year together, have a 'worst pun competition.'

Write your plan:
steps.body:

Not 'go on a date.' Something small, spontaneous, and silly.

questions.label:
What stops you from being playful with your partner?

• You're too tired?
• You think they won't engage?
• You feel silly?
• There's unresolved tension that makes play feel fake?

Name the block. Then ask yourself: is that block real, or is it protecting you from something?

steps.question: Couples who play together have higher relationship satisfaction across all stages of marriage.
feedback: Correct. Research consistently shows that shared play, humor, and lightness are strongly correlated with long-term relationship satisfaction — even more than shared values in some studies.
Actually, yes — studies show that playfulness and shared humor are hallmarks of the happiest couples, from newlyweds to couples married 40+ years.

steps.title: Tomorrow: Physical Intimacy — Reclaiming Touch
steps.body: Play and lightness warm the connection. Tomorrow, we build on that with the physical dimension — touch, affection, and sexual intimacy. No pressure, no performance. Just reconnecting through your bodies.

steps.title: Day Complete
steps.body: Great work today! You're building something real.
